title: ParCC Tutorial Index

Oxford University Press; 2006. Collett D. Modelling Survival Data Medical Research. 3rd ed. Chapman Hall/CRC; 2015. Drummond MF, et al. title: Background Mortality Adjustments
text: Health economic models track cohorts long time horizons (often lifetime). Patients can die disease modelled, also face background (-cause) mortality causes. Census life tables provide general population mortality rates, must adjusted specific patient population. ParCC provides four methods handling background mortality.

title: Background Mortality Adjustments
text: modelling cohort 65-year-old heart failure patients. general population mortality rate age 65 (Indian Census life table) 0.013 per year. Published literature reports heart failure patients Standardized Mortality Ratio (SMR) 2.5 compared general population (Meta-analysis Jhund et al., Eur Heart J 2009).

title: Background Mortality Adjustments
text: rdisease=rpopulation×SMRr_{disease} = r_{population} \times SMR pdisease=1−e−rdisease×tp_{disease} = 1 - e^{-r_{disease} \times t}

title: Background Mortality Adjustments
code:
r_pop <- 0.013   # General population rate, age 65 smr <- 2.5        # From published meta-analysis  r_disease <- r_pop * smr p_disease <- 1 - exp(-r_disease)  cat("Population rate:", r_pop, "
") #> Population rate: 0.013 cat("SMR:", smr, "
") #> SMR: 2.5 cat("Disease-adjusted rate:", round(r_disease, 5), "
") #> Disease-adjusted rate: 0.0325 cat("Annual mortality probability:", round(p_disease, 5), "
") #> Annual mortality probability: 0.03198

previous_headings: Method 2: DEALE (Declining Exponential Approximation of Life Expectancy)
what: The Scenario – Rare Disease with Known Life Expectancy
title: Background Mortality Adjustments
text: Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy, observed mean life expectancy 25 years (registry study). background life expectancy age-sex group 65 years.

previous_headings: Method 2: DEALE (Declining Exponential Approximation of Life Expectancy)
title: Background Mortality Adjustments
text: rdisease=1LEobserved−1LEbackgroundr_{disease} = \frac{1}{LE_{observed}} - \frac{1}{LE_{background}}

previous_headings: Method 2: DEALE (Declining Exponential Approximation of Life Expectancy)
title: Background Mortality Adjustments
code: le_observed <- 25    # Disease-specific life expectancy le_background <- 65  # General population  r_excess <- (1 / le_observed) - (1 / le_background) cat("Excess mortality rate:", round(r_excess, 5), "\n") #> Excess mortality rate: 0.02462 cat("This means patients face an additional", round(r_excess * 1000, 2),     "deaths per 1,000 person-years beyond background\n") #> This means patients face an additional 24.62 deaths per 1,000 person-years beyond background

title: Background Mortality Adjustments
text: Gompertz law describes mortality increases exponentially age, closely matches human mortality patterns age 30: r(age)=α×eβ×ager(age) = \alpha \times e^{\beta \times age} useful need age-specific mortality rates data points life table.

title: Background Mortality Adjustments
code:
# Fit Gompertz from two life table points # Age 60: rate = 0.008 # Age 80: rate = 0.065  r1 <- 0.008; age1 <- 60 r2 <- 0.065; age2 <- 80  beta <- log(r2 / r1) / (age2 - age1) alpha <- r1 / exp(beta * age1)  cat("Gompertz alpha:", format(alpha, scientific = TRUE), "
") #> Gompertz alpha: 1.491488e-05 cat("Gompertz beta:", round(beta, 5), "

") #> Gompertz beta: 0.10475  # Generate age-specific rates ages <- seq(50, 90, by = 5) rates <- alpha * exp(beta * ages) probs <- 1 - exp(-rates)  data.frame(   Age = ages,   Rate = round(rates, 5),   Annual_Prob = round(probs, 5) ) #>   Age    Rate Annual_Prob #> 1  50 0.00281     0.00280 #> 2  55 0.00474     0.00473 #> 3  60 0.00800     0.00797 #> 4  65 0.01351     0.01342 #> 5  70 0.02280     0.02255 #> 6  75 0.03850     0.03777 #> 7  80 0.06500     0.06293 #> 8  85 0.10974     0.10393 #> 9  90 0.18528     0.16913

title: Background Mortality Adjustments
text: ages available life table entries, linear interpolation provides simple estimate: r(age)=r1+(age−age1)×r2−r1age2−age1r(age) = r_1 + (age - age_1) \times \frac{r_2 - r_1}{age_2 - age_1}

title: Background Mortality Adjustments
text: Beck JR, et al. convenient approximation life expectancy (“DEALE”). J Med. 1982;73(6):883-888. Gompertz B. nature function expressive law human mortality. Phil Trans Royal Soc. 1825;115:513-583. Jhund PS, et al. Long-term trends first hospitalization heart failure subsequent survival. Eur Heart J. 2009;30(4):1807-1812.

title: Batch Conversion Workflow
text: populating Markov model, may need convert dozens parameters simultaneously. Rather entering one manually, ParCC’s Bulk Conversion module lets upload CSV, select columns, convert .

title: Batch Conversion Workflow
text: Rate Probability – incidence rates clinical trials Odds Probability – odds ratios logistic regression HR Intervention Probability – applying hazard ratios control arm probabilities

title: Batch Conversion Workflow
text: Create CSV least columns:
code: Endpoint,Control_Prob,Hazard_Ratio,Time_Horizon CV Death,0.0525,0.79,1 MI,0.0643,0.84,1 Stroke,0.0138,1.01,1 Major Bleeding,0.1143,1.04,1 Dyspnea AE,0.0789,1.37,1 All-cause Mortality,0.0595,0.78,1

title: Batch Conversion Workflow
text: Navigate Bulk Conversion Upload CSV (click “Load Sample Dataset” pre-built PLATO example) Select Conversion Type: HR -> Intervention Probability Select Value Column: Control_Prob Select HR Column: Hazard_Ratio Time Source: Constant = 1 (column)

title: Batch Conversion Workflow
text: Click Run Bulk Conversion. output table adds:

title: Batch Conversion Workflow
text: Click Download Results get CSV can paste directly model spreadsheet.

title: Batch Conversion Workflow
text: Notice endpoints HR > 1 (Stroke, Major Bleeding, Dyspnea) show negative ARR, meaning intervention increases event risk – rows correctly show NA NNT.
code: # Simulate what ParCC produces df <- data.frame(   Endpoint = c("CV Death", "MI", "Stroke", "Major Bleeding", "Dyspnea AE", "All-cause Mortality"),   Control_Prob = c(0.0525, 0.0643, 0.0138, 0.1143, 0.0789, 0.0595),   HR = c(0.79, 0.84, 1.01, 1.04, 1.37, 0.78),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  t <- 1  # 1-year horizon  df$Rate_Ctrl <- -log(1 - df$Control_Prob) / t df$Rate_Int <- df$Rate_Ctrl * df$HR df$Interv_Prob <- round(1 - exp(-df$Rate_Int * t), 5) df$ARR <- round(df$Control_Prob - df$Interv_Prob, 5) df$NNT <- ifelse(df$ARR > 0, ceiling(1 / df$ARR), NA)  # Clean display result <- df[, c("Endpoint", "Control_Prob", "HR", "Interv_Prob", "ARR", "NNT")] print(result) #>              Endpoint Control_Prob   HR Interv_Prob      ARR NNT #> 1            CV Death       0.0525 0.79     0.04171  0.01079  93 #> 2                  MI       0.0643 0.84     0.05430  0.01000 100 #> 3              Stroke       0.0138 1.01     0.01394 -0.00014  NA #> 4      Major Bleeding       0.1143 1.04     0.11859 -0.00429  NA #> 5          Dyspnea AE       0.0789 1.37     0.10649 -0.02759  NA #> 6 All-cause Mortality       0.0595 0.78     0.04672  0.01278  79

title: Batch Conversion Workflow
text: Column naming: ParCC auto-detects columns containing “Rate”, “Odds”, “Prob”, “HR”, “Hazard”, “Time” names. Mixed conversions: mix rates probabilities, split CSV two files run separately. sample dataset pre-loaded realistic PLATO trial data – useful learning workflow.

title: Batch Conversion Workflow
text: Wallentin L, et al. Ticagrelor versus clopidogrel patients acute coronary syndromes. N Engl J Med. 2009;361(11):1045-1057. Briggs , et al. Decision Modelling Health Economic Evaluation. Oxford University Press; 2006.

title: Budget Impact Analysis
text: Budget Impact Analysis (BIA) estimates financial consequences adopting new health technology payer’s perspective short time horizon (typically 1-5 years). Unlike cost-effectiveness analysis, BIA focuses affordability, value money. ParCC implements ISPOR BIA Good Practice framework.

previous_headings: Tutorial: 5-Year BIA for a New Oral Anticoagulant
title: Budget Impact Analysis
text: advising state health insurance programme adopting new oral anticoagulant (NOAC) replace warfarin Atrial Fibrillation.

previous_headings: Tutorial: 5-Year BIA for a New Oral Anticoagulant
title: Budget Impact Analysis
text: BIA compares two scenarios: Current scenario: eligible patients receive warfarin. New scenario: proportion (based uptake) switch NOAC; rest stay warfarin. BIt=Ntarget×Uptaket×(Cnew−Ccurrent)×1(1+r)t−1BI_t = N_{target} \times Uptake_t \times (C_{new} - C_{current}) \times \frac{1}{(1+r)^{t-1}}

previous_headings: Tutorial: 5-Year BIA for a New Oral Anticoagulant
title: Budget Impact Analysis
text: Navigate Costs & Outcomes > Budget Impact Analysis. Enter Population = 10,000,000. Enter Prevalence = 0.8%, Eligible = 60%. Enter uptake: 10, 25, 45, 60, 70. Enter Per-Patient Cost (New) = 22,000, (Current) = 8,000. Set Discount Rate = 3%. Click Calculate BIA.

previous_headings: Tutorial: 5-Year BIA for a New Oral Anticoagulant
title: Budget Impact Analysis
text: ParCC produces: Target population: 48,000 eligible patients (fixed year). Year--year table: Shows patients therapy, costs scenarios, incremental budget impact (undiscounted discounted). Stacked bar + line chart: Bars show costs scenario; red line shows incremental budget impact. 5-year cumulative total: discounted sum represents additional budget insurer needs allocate.

previous_headings: Tutorial: 5-Year BIA for a New Oral Anticoagulant
title: Budget Impact Analysis
text: 4,800 patients switch (10% uptake) Incremental cost per patient: INR 22,000 - INR 8,000 = INR 14,000 Year 1 budget impact: 4,800 x INR 14,000 = INR 67.2 million

title: Budget Impact Analysis
text: ISPOR recommends 1-5 years, matching payer’s budget cycle. Longer horizons introduce much uncertainty uptake projections.

title: Budget Impact Analysis
text: Real-world adoption follows S-curve, instant switching. Conservative estimates early years credible. ParCC allows independent specification year.

title: Budget Impact Analysis
text: primary BIA undiscounted (set rate 0%) since payers care actual cash flows. Discounted results can presented secondary analysis consistency CEA.

title: Budget Impact Analysis
text: ParCC uses static population (eligible count year). diseases significant incidence/mortality, dynamic population model may needed – beyond ParCC’s current scope.

title: Budget Impact Analysis
text: ISPOR 2012 Task Force recommends: Use short time horizon matching payer’s budget cycle Model uptake realistically (instant switching) Include direct costs relevant payer perspective Present undiscounted results primary analysis Run scenario analyses uptake rates eligible population Report year--year results, just cumulative totals

title: Budget Impact Analysis
text: Sullivan SD, Mauskopf JA, Augustovski F, et al. Budget impact analysis – principles good practice: report ISPOR 2012 Budget Impact Analysis Good Practice II Task Force. Value Health. 2014;17(1):5-14. Mauskopf JA, Sullivan SD, Annemans L, et al. Principles good practice budget impact analysis: report ISPOR Task Force Good Research Practices. Value Health. 2007;10(5):336-347.

title: Core Conversions: Rates, Odds, and Time Rescaling
text: Health economic models require transition probabilities, clinical literature often reports rates odds. different quantities used interchangeably. vignette explains conversions demonstrates realistic scenarios.

title: Core Conversions: Rates, Odds, and Time Rescaling
text: building Markov model comparing Dabigatran vs Warfarin atrial fibrillation. RE-LY trial (Connolly et al., NEJM 2009) reports incidence rate major bleeding : 3.36 events per 100 patient-years Warfarin arm

title: Core Conversions: Rates, Odds, and Time Rescaling
text: Dividing 3.36 100 gives 0.0336. ignores continuous nature risk – patients bleed early year removed -risk pool, meaning remaining patients face slightly different risk.

title: Core Conversions: Rates, Odds, and Time Rescaling
text: p=1−e−rtp = 1 - e^{-rt} rr instantaneous rate tt time horizon.

title: Core Conversions: Rates, Odds, and Time Rescaling
code:
# RE-LY trial: Warfarin arm major bleeding rate_per_100 <- 3.36 r <- rate_per_100 / 100   # Convert to per-person rate t <- 1                     # 1-year model cycle  p <- 1 - exp(-r * t) cat("Rate (per person-year):", r, "
") #> Rate (per person-year): 0.0336 cat("Annual probability:", round(p, 5), "
") #> Annual probability: 0.03304 cat("Naive division would give:", r, "(overestimates by", round((r - p)/p * 100, 2), "%)
") #> Naive division would give: 0.0336 (overestimates by 1.69 %)

title: Core Conversions: Rates, Odds, and Time Rescaling
text: Navigate Converters > Rate <-> Probability Input Rate = 3.36, Multiplier = Per 100 Time = 1 Result: 0.03304

title: Core Conversions: Rates, Odds, and Time Rescaling
text: building Diabetes model 1-year cycles. UKPDS Risk Engine (Clarke et al., Diabetologia 2004) predicts 10-year probability coronary heart disease 20% 55-year-old male HbA1c 8%.

title: Core Conversions: Rates, Odds, and Time Rescaling
text: Dividing 0.20 10 gives 0.02 (2% per year). risk compounds: survive Year 1, face risk Year 2. correct conversion accounts compounding.

title: Core Conversions: Rates, Odds, and Time Rescaling
text: pnew=1−(1−pold)tnew/toldp_{new} = 1 - (1 - p_{old})^{t_{new}/t_{old}}

title: Core Conversions: Rates, Odds, and Time Rescaling
code:
p_10yr <- 0.20 t_old <- 10 t_new <- 1  p_1yr <- 1 - (1 - p_10yr)^(t_new / t_old) cat("10-year probability:", p_10yr, "
") #> 10-year probability: 0.2 cat("Correct 1-year probability:", round(p_1yr, 5), "
") #> Correct 1-year probability: 0.02207 cat("Naive (divide by 10):", p_10yr / 10, "
") #> Naive (divide by 10): 0.02 cat("Correct value is", round((p_1yr - 0.02)/0.02 * 100, 1), "% higher
") #> Correct value is 10.3 % higher

title: Core Conversions: Rates, Odds, and Time Rescaling
text: Navigate Converters > Time Rescaling Input Probability = 0.20, Original Time = 10 Years New Time = 1 Year Result: 0.02206

title: Core Conversions: Rates, Odds, and Time Rescaling
text: logistic regression predicting post-surgical infection reports odds ratio 2.5 patients diabetes (vs diabetes). baseline infection probability (diabetes) 8%.

title: Core Conversions: Rates, Odds, and Time Rescaling
text: p=Odds1+Oddsp = \frac{\text{Odds}}{1 + \text{Odds}}

title: Core Conversions: Rates, Odds, and Time Rescaling
code:
p_baseline <- 0.08 odds_baseline <- p_baseline / (1 - p_baseline) odds_diabetes <- odds_baseline * 2.5 p_diabetes <- odds_diabetes / (1 + odds_diabetes)  cat("Baseline odds:", round(odds_baseline, 4), "
") #> Baseline odds: 0.087 cat("Diabetes odds (OR=2.5):", round(odds_diabetes, 4), "
") #> Diabetes odds (OR=2.5): 0.2174 cat("Diabetes probability:", round(p_diabetes, 4), "
") #> Diabetes probability: 0.1786

title: Core Conversions: Rates, Odds, and Time Rescaling
text: Sonnenberg FA, Beck JR. Markov models medical decision making: practical guide. Med Decis Making. 1993;13(4):322-338. Fleurence RL, Hollenbeak CS. Rates probabilities economic modelling. Pharmacoeconomics. 2007;25(1):3-12. Connolly SJ, et al. Dabigatran versus warfarin patients atrial fibrillation. N Engl J Med. 2009;361(12):1139-1151. Clarke PM, et al. model estimate lifetime health outcomes patients Type 2 diabetes. Diabetologia. 2004;47(10):1747-1759.

title: Dirichlet Distribution & Log-Logistic Survival
text: Standard PSA uses Beta (utilities) Gamma (costs). Two common modelling situations need specialised distributions: multinomial transition probabilities require Dirichlet maintain row-sum constraints, diseases hump-shaped hazards need Log-Logistic survival model.

previous_headings: Part A: Dirichlet for Transition Matrices
title: Dirichlet Distribution & Log-Logistic Survival
text: building 3-state Markov model Chronic Kidney Disease (Stable -> Progressed -> Dead). cohort 200 patients observed 1 year starting “Stable”: 150 remained Stable 35 progressed CKD Stage 4 15 died three probabilities (0.75, 0.175, 0.075) must sum 1.0 every PSA iteration. sample independently using three Beta distributions, almost never sum 1 – breaking model.

previous_headings: Part A: Dirichlet for Transition Matrices
title: Dirichlet Distribution & Log-Logistic Survival
text: Dirichlet distribution multivariate generalisation Beta. parameters observed counts: 𝛂=(150,35,15)\boldsymbol{\alpha} = (150, 35, 15) sample Dirichlet complete probability vector sums exactly 1.0.

previous_headings: Part A: Dirichlet for Transition Matrices
title: Dirichlet Distribution & Log-Logistic Survival
text: ParCC uses standard algorithm: Draw Xi∼Gamma(αi,1)X_i \sim \text{Gamma}(\alpha_i, 1) state Compute pi=Xi/∑jXjp_i = X_i / \sum_j X_j resulting (p1,p2,p3)(p_1, p_2, p_3) Dirichlet-distributed sums 1

previous_headings: Part A: Dirichlet for Transition Matrices
title: Dirichlet Distribution & Log-Logistic Survival
text: Navigate Uncertainty (PSA) select Dirichlet (Multinomial). Enter counts: 150, 35, 15. Enter labels: Stable, Progressed, Dead. Click Fit & Sample. ParCC displays Dirichlet parameters, mean proportions, bar chart sampled proportions, ready--use R code snippet PSA loop.

title: Dirichlet Distribution & Log-Logistic Survival
text: modelling recovery hip replacement surgery. hazard revision : Low immediately surgery (close monitoring) Peaks around year 5-7 (implant loosening) Declines year 10 (survivors well-fixed implants) Neither Exponential (constant hazard) Weibull (monotonic hazard) can capture hump-shaped pattern.

title: Dirichlet Distribution & Log-Logistic Survival
text: survival function : S(t)=11+(t/α)βS(t) = \frac{1}{1 + (t/\alpha)^\beta} hazard function : h(t)=(β/α)(t/α)β−11+(t/α)βh(t) = \frac{(\beta/\alpha)(t/\alpha)^{\beta-1}}{1 + (t/\alpha)^\beta} β>1\beta > 1, hazard rises peak falls – exactly hump shape needed.

title: Dirichlet Distribution & Log-Logistic Survival
text: published Kaplan-Meier curve, identify two time-survival points: Point 1: Year 5, implant survival = 92% Point 2: Year 15, implant survival = 78% Navigate Survival Curves > Fit Survival Curve. Select method: Log-Logistic (2 Time Points). Enter values. ParCC solves alpha (scale) beta (shape). Verify beta > 1 output confirm expected hump-shaped hazard.

title: Dirichlet Distribution & Log-Logistic Survival
text: ParCC uses log-odds transformation. Since S(t)=1/(1+(t/α)β)S(t) = 1/(1 + (t/\alpha)^\beta): ln(1−S(t)S(t))=βln(t)−βln(α)\ln\left(\frac{1 - S(t)}{S(t)}\right) = \beta \ln(t) - \beta \ln(\alpha) Two points yield two equations, solved alpha beta.

title: Dirichlet Distribution & Log-Logistic Survival
text: parametric survival models, extrapolation beyond observed data requires clinical justification. Log-Logistic’s long tail means predicts higher long-term survival Weibull – validate clinical expectations.

title: Dirichlet Distribution & Log-Logistic Survival
text: Briggs , Claxton K, Sculpher M. Decision Modelling Health Economic Evaluation. Oxford University Press; 2006. Chapter 4: Probabilistic Sensitivity Analysis. Collett D. Modelling Survival Data Medical Research. 3rd ed. Chapman & Hall/CRC; 2015. Chapter 5: Log-Logistic Models. NICE Decision Support Unit Technical Support Document 14: Survival Analysis Economic Evaluations Alongside Clinical Trials. 2013.

title: Economic Evaluation: ICER, NMB, and Value-Based Pricing
text: ICER=ΔCostΔEffect=Cintervention−CcomparatorEintervention−EcomparatorICER = \frac{\Delta Cost}{\Delta Effect} = \frac{C_{intervention} - C_{comparator}}{E_{intervention} - E_{comparator}}

title: Economic Evaluation: ICER, NMB, and Value-Based Pricing
text: trial-based economic evaluation compares new DPP-4 inhibitor metformin monotherapy 5 years:
code:
c_new <- 142000; c_old <- 85000 e_new <- 4.15; e_old <- 3.82  delta_c <- c_new - c_old delta_e <- e_new - e_old icer <- delta_c / delta_e  cat("Incremental Cost: INR", format(delta_c, big.mark = ","), "
") #> Incremental Cost: INR 57,000 cat("Incremental QALYs:", delta_e, "
") #> Incremental QALYs: 0.33 cat("ICER: INR", format(round(icer), big.mark = ","), "per QALY
") #> ICER: INR 172,727 per QALY

title: Economic Evaluation: ICER, NMB, and Value-Based Pricing
text: intervention falls NE quadrant (costly, effective). Compare ICER Willingness--Pay (WTP) threshold: ICER < WTP: Cost-effective ICER > WTP: cost-effective

title: Economic Evaluation: ICER, NMB, and Value-Based Pricing
text: iNMB=(ΔE×WTP)−ΔCiNMB = (\Delta E \times WTP) - \Delta C iNMB > 0 means cost-effective given WTP.
code:
wtp <- 100000  # INR per QALY (HTAIn threshold)  inmb <- (delta_e * wtp) - delta_c cat("iNMB at WTP INR 1,00,000/QALY: INR", format(round(inmb), big.mark = ","), "
") #> iNMB at WTP INR 1,00,000/QALY: INR -24,000  if (inmb > 0) {   cat("Decision: Cost-effective (iNMB > 0)
") } else {   cat("Decision: NOT cost-effective (iNMB < 0)
") } #> Decision: NOT cost-effective (iNMB < 0)

what: The Scenario – SepsiQuick (Point-of-Care Test for Sepsis)
title: Economic Evaluation: ICER, NMB, and Value-Based Pricing
text: pricing new rapid diagnostic test sepsis. evidence: Clinical benefit: 0.02 QALYs per patient (faster treatment initiation) Comparator cost: INR 500 (existing lab-based test) Associated costs: INR 200 (nurse time bedside testing) WTP threshold: INR 1,00,000 per QALY

title: Economic Evaluation: ICER, NMB, and Value-Based Pricing
text: Cmax=(ΔE×WTP)+CcomparatorC_{max} = (\Delta E \times WTP) + C_{comparator} Pmax=Cmax−CassociatedNP_{max} = \frac{C_{max} - C_{associated}}{N}

title: Economic Evaluation: ICER, NMB, and Value-Based Pricing
code:
delta_e_test <- 0.02 wtp_threshold <- 100000 c_comparator <- 500 c_associated <- 200 n_units <- 1  c_max <- (delta_e_test * wtp_threshold) + c_comparator p_max <- (c_max - c_associated) / n_units  cat("Clinical Value: INR", format(delta_e_test * wtp_threshold, big.mark = ","), "
") #> Clinical Value: INR 2,000 cat("+ Savings from replacing old test: INR", format(c_comparator, big.mark = ","), "
") #> + Savings from replacing old test: INR 500 cat("= Total Headroom: INR", format(c_max, big.mark = ","), "
") #> = Total Headroom: INR 2,500 cat("- Nurse time: INR", format(c_associated, big.mark = ","), "
") #> - Nurse time: INR 200 cat("= Maximum Justifiable Price: INR", format(p_max, big.mark = ","), "
") #> = Maximum Justifiable Price: INR 2,300

what: What If You Price Above the Headroom?
title: Economic Evaluation: ICER, NMB, and Value-Based Pricing
code:
proposed_price <- 3000 overpriced_by <- ((proposed_price - p_max) / p_max) * 100  if (proposed_price > p_max) {   cat("At INR", format(proposed_price, big.mark = ","),       "the product is", round(overpriced_by, 1), "% above the maximum justifiable price.
")   cat("Recommendation: Reduce price by INR",       format(proposed_price - p_max, big.mark = ","), "
") } else {   cat("Price is within the cost-effective range.
") } #> At INR 3,000 the product is 30.4 % above the maximum justifiable price. #> Recommendation: Reduce price by INR 700

title: Economic Evaluation: ICER, NMB, and Value-Based Pricing
text: Drummond MF, et al. Methods Economic Evaluation Health Care Programmes. 4th ed. Oxford University Press; 2015. Cosh E, et al. value ‘innovation headroom’. Value Health. 2007;10(4):312-315. HTAIn Methods Guide. Department Health Research, Ministry Health & Family Welfare, Government India.

title: Getting Started with ParCC
text: ParCC (Parameter Converter & Calculator) decision support tool Health Technology Assessment (HTA). bridges gap reported clinical statistics specific input parameters required health economic models Markov cohort models, decision trees, partitioned survival models.

title: Getting Started with ParCC
text: Install ParCC GitHub:
code: # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("drpakhare/ParCC")

title: Getting Started with ParCC
text: opens browser-based Shiny application calculation tools.
code: library(ParCC) run_app()

title: Getting Started with ParCC
text: Reproducibility critical HTA. Every parameter model must traceable source methodology. ParCC acts digital logbook. 4-Step Workflow: Label : calculating, type specific name ‘Label’ box (e.g., “PFS Control Arm” “Cost Surgery - AIIMS 2023”). Calculate & Log: Click blue action button. calculates result saves session log timestamp. Review: Navigate Report tab see table logged parameters. Export: Download HTML Report. document serves Technical Appendix manuscript HTA dossier, complete formulas references.

title: Getting Started with ParCC
text: ParCC provides 12 calculation modules organized four categories:

title: Getting Started with ParCC
text: Converters – Rate/Odds Probability, Time Rescaling HR Converter – Apply Hazard Ratios trials convert control probabilities intervention probabilities Bulk Conversion – Upload CSV convert entire column Survival Analysis – Fit Exponential Weibull curves summary data Background Mortality – SMR adjustment, Gompertz fitting, DEALE, interpolation

title: Getting Started with ParCC
text: PSA – Fit Beta, Gamma, LogNormal distributions Probabilistic Sensitivity Analysis

title: Getting Started with ParCC
text: Financial – Inflation adjustment discounting calculators

title: Getting Started with ParCC

title: HR-Based Probability Converter
text: One common tasks health economic modelling converting control group probability intervention group probability using Hazard Ratio (HR) clinical trial. arises whenever: baseline event rates registry observational study trial reports relative treatment effects HRs need transition probabilities arms Markov model common mistake multiply probability directly HR (.e., pint=pctrl×HRp_{int} = p_{ctrl} \times HR). mathematically incorrect HR operates instantaneous rate, cumulative probability.

title: HR-Based Probability Converter
text: proportional hazards assumption (HR constant time): Step 1: Convert control probability rate rcontrol=−ln(1−pcontrol)tr_{control} = -\frac{\ln(1 - p_{control})}{t} Step 2: Apply Hazard Ratio rintervention=rcontrol×HRr_{intervention} = r_{control} \times HR Step 3: Convert back probability pintervention=1−e−rintervention×tcyclep_{intervention} = 1 - e^{-r_{intervention} \times t_{cycle}}

what: Worked Example 1: PLATO Trial (Ticagrelor vs Aspirin)
title: HR-Based Probability Converter
text: PLATO trial (Wallentin et al., NEJM 2009) compared Ticagrelor Aspirin Acute Coronary Syndrome. Key results 12 months:

previous_headings: Worked Example 1: PLATO Trial (Ticagrelor vs Aspirin)
title: HR-Based Probability Converter
code:
# PLATO trial - CV Death p_control <- 0.0525   # 5.25% at 12 months hr <- 0.79             # Ticagrelor vs Aspirin t <- 1                 # 1 year  # Step 1: Probability -> Rate r_control <- -log(1 - p_control) / t cat("Step 1 - Control rate:", round(r_control, 5), "per year
") #> Step 1 - Control rate: 0.05393 per year  # Step 2: Apply HR r_intervention <- r_control * hr cat("Step 2 - Intervention rate:", round(r_intervention, 5), "per year
") #> Step 2 - Intervention rate: 0.0426 per year  # Step 3: Rate -> Probability p_intervention <- 1 - exp(-r_intervention * t) cat("Step 3 - Intervention probability:", round(p_intervention, 5), "

") #> Step 3 - Intervention probability: 0.04171  # Compare with naive method p_naive <- p_control * hr cat("Correct method:", round(p_intervention, 5), "
") #> Correct method: 0.04171 cat("Naive (p x HR):", round(p_naive, 5), "
") #> Naive (p x HR): 0.04147 cat("Difference:", round((p_intervention - p_naive) * 10000, 2), "per 10,000 patients
") #> Difference: 2.34 per 10,000 patients

previous_headings: Worked Example 1: PLATO Trial (Ticagrelor vs Aspirin)
title: HR-Based Probability Converter
code:
# Apply CI bounds hr_low <- 0.69 hr_high <- 0.91  p_low <- 1 - exp(-r_control * hr_low * t) p_high <- 1 - exp(-r_control * hr_high * t)  cat("Intervention probability: ", round(p_intervention, 5),     " (95% CI:", round(p_low, 5), "to", round(p_high, 5), ")
") #> Intervention probability:  0.04171  (95% CI: 0.03653 to 0.04789 )  # Clinical impact arr <- p_control - p_intervention nnt <- ceiling(1 / arr) cat("ARR:", round(arr * 100, 2), "%
") #> ARR: 1.08 % cat("NNT:", nnt, "
") #> NNT: 93

title: HR-Based Probability Converter
text: error naive multiplication grows higher event rates. Consider oncology model control arm 2-year mortality 40%:
code:
p_control_onc <- 0.40 hr_onc <- 0.75 t_onc <- 2  # Correct method r_ctrl <- -log(1 - p_control_onc) / t_onc r_int <- r_ctrl * hr_onc p_int_correct <- 1 - exp(-r_int * t_onc)  # Naive method p_int_naive <- p_control_onc * hr_onc  cat("Control 2-year mortality:", p_control_onc, "
") #> Control 2-year mortality: 0.4 cat("HR:", hr_onc, "

") #> HR: 0.75 cat("Correct intervention probability:", round(p_int_correct, 4), "
") #> Correct intervention probability: 0.3183 cat("Naive (p x HR):", round(p_int_naive, 4), "
") #> Naive (p x HR): 0.3 cat("Error:", round(abs(p_int_correct - p_int_naive) * 100, 2), "percentage points
") #> Error: 1.83 percentage points cat("This affects", round(abs(p_int_correct - p_int_naive) * 10000), "patients per 10,000
") #> This affects 183 patients per 10,000

what: Worked Example 3: Rescaling to Monthly Cycles
title: HR-Based Probability Converter
text: Often need monthly transition probabilities Markov model. can combine HR conversion time rescaling:
code: # PLATO CV Death, but for a monthly model p_annual_ctrl <- 0.0525 hr <- 0.79  # Convert annual probability to rate r_ctrl <- -log(1 - p_annual_ctrl) / 1 r_int <- r_ctrl * hr  # Convert to monthly probabilities t_month <- 1/12 p_monthly_ctrl <- 1 - exp(-r_ctrl * t_month) p_monthly_int <- 1 - exp(-r_int * t_month)  cat("Monthly control probability:", round(p_monthly_ctrl, 6), "\n") #> Monthly control probability: 0.004484 cat("Monthly intervention probability:", round(p_monthly_int, 6), "\n") #> Monthly intervention probability: 0.003544

title: HR-Based Probability Converter
text: Proportional Hazards: HR assumed constant time. Kaplan-Meier curves cross, assumption violated alternative methods (e.g., piecewise HRs) considered. Exponential within cycle: conversion assumes exponential survival within model cycle. standard practice short cycles (1 month, 1 year) may less appropriate long cycles. Independent events: conversion treats event independently. competing risks models, additional adjustments may needed.

title: OR <-> RR Conversion & Effect Size Transformations
text: Network Meta-Analysis (NMA) requires treatment effects common scale. However, trials report results Odds Ratios, Relative Risks, Standardised Mean Differences depending outcome type. ParCC provides bidirectional conversions unify metrics pooling.

previous_headings: Tutorial: Preparing Data for an NMA in Depression
title: OR <-> RR Conversion & Effect Size Transformations
text: conducting NMA comparing three antidepressants. systematic review found: Trial (Drug vs Placebo): = 1.85 “Response” (>=50% reduction HAM-D). Baseline response placebo arm = 30%. Trial B (Drug vs Placebo): RR = 1.42 “Response”. Trial C (Drug vs Placebo): Reports continuous outcome: SMD = 0.45 (Cohen’s d) HAM-D score. pool single NMA, need three scale.

previous_headings: Tutorial: Preparing Data for an NMA in Depression
what: Step 1: Convert OR to RR (Zhang & Yu Method)
title: OR <-> RR Conversion & Effect Size Transformations
text: Zhang & Yu (1998) formula accounts baseline risk: RR=OR1−p0+p0×ORRR = \frac{}{1 - p_0 + p_0 \times } p0p_0 baseline risk control group. ParCC: Navigate Convert > Rate <-> Probability > <-> RR tab. Select direction: -> RR. Input = 1.85, Baseline Risk = 0.30. Result: RR ~ 1.42.

previous_headings: Tutorial: Preparing Data for an NMA in Depression
title: OR <-> RR Conversion & Effect Size Transformations
text: outcome rare (<10%), ~ RR conversion wouldn’t matter. 30% baseline risk, 1.85 overstates effect compared RR 1.42. Failing convert bias NMA.

previous_headings: Tutorial: Preparing Data for an NMA in Depression
what: Step 2: Convert SMD to log(OR) (Chinn Method)
title: OR <-> RR Conversion & Effect Size Transformations
text: Chinn (2000) approximation uses logistic distribution: ln()=SMD×π3≈SMD×1.8138\ln() = SMD \times \frac{\pi}{\sqrt{3}} \approx SMD \times 1.8138 ParCC: Switch Effect Size Conversions tab. Select direction: SMD -> log(). Input SMD = 0.45. Result: log() = 0.816, .e. ~ 2.26.

previous_headings: Tutorial: Preparing Data for an NMA in Depression
title: OR <-> RR Conversion & Effect Size Transformations
text: bring Trial C onto RR scale (matching Trials B): ln(RR)=ln(eln()1−p0+p0×eln())\ln(RR) = \ln\left(\frac{e^{\ln()}}{1 - p_0 + p_0 \times e^{\ln()}}\right) ParCC chains Chinn Zhang & Yu methods automatically.

title: OR <-> RR Conversion & Effect Size Transformations
text: baseline risk low (p0<0.10p_0 < 0.10), ~ RR mathematically. ParCC displays note approximation holds. common outcomes (>10%), always convert explicitly.

title: PPP Currency Conversion & WTP Thresholds
text: comparing health technology costs across countries, market exchange rates misleading – reflect capital flows, actual purchasing power money economy. Purchasing Power Parity (PPP) conversion factors adjust price-level differences, making costs genuinely comparable. ParCC ships World Bank PPP data 30 countries automatically assesses costs -CHOICE willingness--pay thresholds.

previous_headings: Tutorial: Is a US Drug Affordable in India?

text: new oncology drug priced $50,000 per year United States. advising HTAIn whether price justifiable Indian market.

previous_headings: Tutorial: Is a US Drug Affordable in India?
what: Why Market Exchange Rates Are Wrong
title: PPP Currency Conversion & WTP Thresholds
text: 2022 market rate ~INR 78.6 per USD: $50,000×78.6=INR39,30,000\$50{,}000 \times 78.6 = INR 39{,}30{,}000 dramatically overstates burden, goods services much cheaper India. doctor’s consultation, hospital bed-day, meal costs far less India US.

title: PPP Currency Conversion & WTP Thresholds
text: PPP conversion uses international dollars intermediate currency: CtargetPPP=CsourcePPPsource×PPPtargetC_{target}^{PPP} = \frac{C_{source}}{PPP_{source}} \times PPP_{target} Step 1: Convert US dollars international dollars: $50,000÷1.00=Int$50,000\$50{,}000 \div 1.00 = \text{Int}\$50{,}000 (US PPP factor 1.00 definition.) Step 2: Convert international dollars Indian rupees: Int$50,000×23.2=INR11,60,000\text{Int}\$50{,}000 \times 23.2 = INR 11{,}60{,}000

what: Why PSA Needs the Right Distribution
title: Parameterizing Distributions for PSA
text: Probabilistic Sensitivity Analysis (PSA) requires sampling model parameters appropriate distributions. Using wrong distribution can produce impossible values (e.g., probabilities > 1 negative costs) bias results.

what: The Scenario – EQ-5D Utility in COPD
title: Parameterizing Distributions for PSA
text: quality--life study COPD (Rutten-van Molken et al., Chest 2006) reports EQ-5D utility “Moderate COPD (GOLD Stage II)” : Mean = 0.76, Standard Error = 0.03

title: Parameterizing Distributions for PSA
text: α=μ(μ(1−μ)SE2−1)\alpha = \mu \left(\frac{\mu(1-\mu)}{SE^2} - 1\right) β=(1−μ)(μ(1−μ)SE2−1)\beta = (1-\mu) \left(\frac{\mu(1-\mu)}{SE^2} - 1\right)

title: Parameterizing Distributions for PSA
text: values bounded 0 1, required utilities.
code:
mu <- 0.76 se <- 0.03  common <- (mu * (1 - mu)) / se^2 - 1 alpha <- mu * common beta_param <- (1 - mu) * common  cat("Alpha:", round(alpha, 2), "
") #> Alpha: 153.27 cat("Beta:", round(beta_param, 2), "
") #> Beta: 48.4  # Verify: sample and check set.seed(42) samples <- rbeta(10000, alpha, beta_param) cat("
Verification (10,000 samples):
") #>  #> Verification (10,000 samples): cat("Mean:", round(mean(samples), 4), "(target:", mu, ")
") #> Mean: 0.7601 (target: 0.76 ) cat("SE:", round(sd(samples), 4), "(target:", se, ")
") #> SE: 0.0303 (target: 0.03 ) cat("Range:", round(range(samples), 4), "
") #> Range: 0.6379 0.86

what: The Scenario – Surgery Cost (AIIMS Costing Study)
title: Parameterizing Distributions for PSA
text: micro-costing study reports cost CABG surgery : Mean = INR 2,50,000, Standard Error = INR 50,000

title: Parameterizing Distributions for PSA
text: k=μ2SE2,θ=SE2μk = \frac{\mu^2}{SE^2}, \quad \theta = \frac{SE^2}{\mu}

title: Parameterizing Distributions for PSA
code:
mu_cost <- 250000 se_cost <- 50000  k <- mu_cost^2 / se_cost^2 theta <- se_cost^2 / mu_cost  cat("Shape (k):", round(k, 2), "
") #> Shape (k): 25 cat("Scale (theta):", round(theta, 2), "
") #> Scale (theta): 10000  # Verify set.seed(42) samples_cost <- rgamma(10000, shape = k, scale = theta) cat("
Verification (10,000 samples):
") #>  #> Verification (10,000 samples): cat("Mean: INR", format(round(mean(samples_cost)), big.mark = ","), "(target:", format(mu_cost, big.mark = ","), ")
") #> Mean: INR 249,971 (target: 250,000 ) cat("SE: INR", format(round(sd(samples_cost)), big.mark = ","), "(target:", format(se_cost, big.mark = ","), ")
") #> SE: INR 50,462 (target: 50,000 ) cat("Min: INR", format(round(min(samples_cost)), big.mark = ","), "(always positive)
") #> Min: INR 90,175 (always positive)

previous_headings: Tutorial 3: Hazard Ratios with LogNormal Distribution
what: The Scenario – HR from Network Meta-Analysis
title: Parameterizing Distributions for PSA
text: network meta-analysis reports HR Drug vs placebo : HR = 0.72, 95% CI: 0.58 0.89

previous_headings: Tutorial 3: Hazard Ratios with LogNormal Distribution
title: Parameterizing Distributions for PSA
code:
hr_mean <- 0.72 hr_low <- 0.58 hr_high <- 0.89  mu_log <- log(hr_mean) se_log <- (log(hr_high) - log(hr_low)) / (2 * 1.96) sigma2_log <- se_log^2  cat("mu_log:", round(mu_log, 4), "
") #> mu_log: -0.3285 cat("sigma_log:", round(se_log, 4), "
") #> sigma_log: 0.1092  # Verify set.seed(42) samples_hr <- rlnorm(10000, meanlog = mu_log, sdlog = se_log) cat("
Verification (10,000 samples):
") #>  #> Verification (10,000 samples): cat("Median HR:", round(median(samples_hr), 3), "(target:", hr_mean, ")
") #> Median HR: 0.72 (target: 0.72 ) cat("2.5th percentile:", round(quantile(samples_hr, 0.025), 3), "(target:", hr_low, ")
") #> 2.5th percentile: 0.58 (target: 0.58 ) cat("97.5th percentile:", round(quantile(samples_hr, 0.975), 3), "(target:", hr_high, ")
") #> 97.5th percentile: 0.892 (target: 0.89 )

what: When SE is Missing: The Rule of 4
title: Parameterizing Distributions for PSA
text: range (min, max) available instead SE, estimate: SE≈High−Low4SE \approx \frac{High - Low}{4} assumes range covers approximately 95% distribution (.e., +/-2 SE).
code: # Example: cost range INR 1,50,000 to INR 3,50,000 cost_low <- 150000 cost_high <- 350000 se_approx <- (cost_high - cost_low) / 4  cat("Estimated SE:", format(se_approx, big.mark = ","), "\n") #> Estimated SE: 50,000

title: Parameterizing Distributions for PSA
text: Briggs , Claxton K, Sculpher M. Decision Modelling Health Economic Evaluation. Oxford University Press; 2006. Chapter 4. Rutten-van Molken MP, et al. Modelling 5-year cost effectiveness tiotropium. Chest. 2006;130(5):1549.

title: Survival Extrapolation from Published Curves
text: Clinical trials typically follow patients 2-5 years, health economic models often require lifetime projections (20-40 years). Individual Patient Data (IPD) unavailable, must extract information published Kaplan-Meier curves fit parametric distributions.

title: Survival Extrapolation from Published Curves
text: exponential distribution assumes constant hazard – risk event every time point. rarely true biologically, can reasonable approximation : median survival reported Kaplan-Meier curve appears roughly linear log scale

title: Survival Extrapolation from Published Curves
text: Phase III trial Bevacizumab + FOLFOX reports median Overall Survival 21.3 months control arm (Hurwitz et al., NEJM 2004).

title: Survival Extrapolation from Published Curves
code: median_os <- 21.3  # months  # Hazard rate from median lambda <- log(2) / median_os cat("Hazard rate (lambda):", round(lambda, 5), "per month\n") #> Hazard rate (lambda): 0.03254 per month  # Survival function: S(t) = exp(-lambda * t) t_seq <- c(6, 12, 24, 36, 60) surv <- exp(-lambda * t_seq)  data.frame(   Month = t_seq,   Survival = round(surv, 3),   Event_Prob = round(1 - surv, 3) ) #>   Month Survival Event_Prob #> 1     6    0.823      0.177 #> 2    12    0.677      0.323 #> 3    24    0.458      0.542 #> 4    36    0.310      0.690 #> 5    60    0.142      0.858

title: Survival Extrapolation from Published Curves
text: Weibull distribution allows hazard increase decrease time (monotonically). flexible exponential frequently used oncology HTA.

title: Survival Extrapolation from Published Curves
text: published Kaplan-Meier curve nivolumab squamous NSCLC shows: 12 months, survival approximately 42% 24 months, survival approximately 23%

title: Survival Extrapolation from Published Curves
text: Weibull survival function : S(t)=e−λtγS(t) = e^{-\lambda t^{\gamma}} Using log-log transformation: ln(−ln(S(t)))=ln(λ)+γln(t)\ln(-\ln(S(t))) = \ln(\lambda) + \gamma \ln(t) two points, solve system two linear equations.

title: Survival Extrapolation from Published Curves
code:
# Two points from the KM curve t1 <- 12; s1 <- 0.42 t2 <- 24; s2 <- 0.23  # Log-log transformation y1 <- log(-log(s1)); x1 <- log(t1) y2 <- log(-log(s2)); x2 <- log(t2)  # Solve for shape (gamma) and scale (lambda) gamma <- (y2 - y1) / (x2 - x1) lambda <- exp(y1 - gamma * x1)  cat("Weibull Shape (gamma):", round(gamma, 4), "
") #> Weibull Shape (gamma): 0.7606 cat("Weibull Scale (lambda):", format(lambda, scientific = TRUE, digits = 4), "
") #> Weibull Scale (lambda): 1.311e-01  # Generate survival table t_seq <- seq(0, 60, by = 6) surv <- exp(-lambda * t_seq^gamma)  # Transition probabilities (monthly) t_monthly <- 0:60 s_monthly <- exp(-lambda * t_monthly^gamma) tp <- c(1, s_monthly[-1] / s_monthly[-length(s_monthly)])  cat("
Survival projections:
") #>  #> Survival projections: data.frame(   Month = seq(0, 60, by = 6),   Survival = round(exp(-lambda * seq(0, 60, by = 6)^gamma), 3) ) #>    Month Survival #> 1      0    1.000 #> 2      6    0.599 #> 3     12    0.420 #> 4     18    0.307 #> 5     24    0.230 #> 6     30    0.175 #> 7     36    0.135 #> 8     42    0.105 #> 9     48    0.083 #> 10    54    0.066 #> 11    60    0.052

title: Survival Extrapolation from Published Curves
text: γ>1\gamma > 1: hazard increasing time (common cancer) γ=1\gamma = 1: constant hazard (reduces exponential) γ<1\gamma < 1: hazard decreasing time (common post-surgery)

title: Survival Extrapolation from Published Curves
text: methods produce cycle-specific transition probabilities Markov model:
code: # Generate annual transition probabilities from Weibull cycles <- 0:10 s_t <- exp(-lambda * (cycles * 12)^gamma)  # Convert years to months for calculation tp <- c(1, s_t[-1] / s_t[-length(s_t)])  trace <- data.frame(   Year = cycles,   Survival = round(s_t, 4),   Annual_TP = round(tp, 4),   Cumulative_Death = round(1 - s_t, 4) ) print(trace) #>    Year Survival Annual_TP Cumulative_Death #> 1     0   1.0000    1.0000           0.0000 #> 2     1   0.4200    0.4200           0.5800 #> 3     2   0.2300    0.5476           0.7700 #> 4     3   0.1353    0.5881           0.8647 #> 5     4   0.0829    0.6131           0.9171 #> 6     5   0.0523    0.6309           0.9477 #> 7     6   0.0337    0.6448           0.9663 #> 8     7   0.0221    0.6560           0.9779 #> 9     8   0.0147    0.6654           0.9853 #> 10    9   0.0099    0.6735           0.9901 #> 11   10   0.0067    0.6805           0.9933

what: Parameter Converter and Calculator for Health Technology Assessment
title: Parameter Converter and Calculator for Health Technology Assessment
text: ParCC interactive Shiny application Health Technology Assessment (HTA) parameter estimation. bridges gap clinical evidence economic models automating complex parameter transformations full formula documentation literature citations. Documentation: https://drpakhare.github.io/ParCC/

title: Parameter Converter and Calculator for Health Technology Assessment
text: can install ParCC GitHub :
code: # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("drpakhare/ParCC")

title: Parameter Converter and Calculator for Health Technology Assessment
code: library(ParCC) run_app()

title: Parameter Converter and Calculator for Health Technology Assessment
text: Core Conversions – Rate Probability, Odds Probability, Time Rescaling, RR (Zhang & Yu), Effect Size (Chinn). HR Converter – HR--probability, multi-HR comparison, NNT/NNH calculator, Log-rank HR (Peto). Survival Extrapolation – Exponential, Weibull, Log-Logistic fitting published KM data. Background Mortality – SMR adjustment, Gompertz fitting, DEALE, linear interpolation. PSA Distributions – Beta, Gamma, LogNormal (Method Moments), Dirichlet (multinomial). Economic Evaluation – ICER, Net Monetary Benefit, Value-Based Pricing (headroom analysis). Budget Impact Analysis – 5-year BIA framework population uptake curves discounting (ISPOR). PPP Currency Converter – Purchasing Power Parity conversion across 30 countries -CHOICE WTP thresholds. Global Currency Selector – INR, USD, EUR, GBP, JPY, ; economic modules update automatically. Batch Processing – Bulk rate, odds, HR-based conversions CSV upload/download. Lab Notebook – Generates HTML report methodology citations every session.

title: Parameter Converter and Calculator for Health Technology Assessment
text: Budget Impact Analysis (ISPOR framework) PPP Currency Converter -CHOICE WTP thresholds -RR effect size conversions NMA preparation NNT/NNH calculator Log-rank HR (Peto) Dirichlet distribution Markov transition matrices Log-Logistic survival non-monotonic hazard support Annuity / PV stream calculator Interactive clickable tool overview Home page 5 new worked-example vignettes

what: ParCC: Parameter Converter and Calculator for Health Technology Assessment — ParCC-package
title: ParCC: Parameter Converter and Calculator for Health Technology Assessment — ParCC-package
text: interactive 'shiny' application Health Technology Assessment (HTA) parameter estimation. Converts rates, probabilities, odds, hazard ratios; extrapolates survival curves (Exponential, Weibull, Log-Logistic); fits PSA distributions (Beta, Gamma, LogNormal, Dirichlet) via method moments; calculates ICERs, net monetary benefit, value-based pricing, budget impact; adjusts costs inflation, discounting, purchasing power parity (PPP) across 30 countries; adjusts background mortality using life-table methods. Designed researchers building cost-effectiveness budget-impact models need auditable, formula-documented parameter transformations. Methods include Zhang Yu (1998) doi:10.1001/jama.280.19.1690  --RR conversion Chinn (2000, Statistics Medicine, 19, 3127-3131) effect-size transformations.

title: ParCC: Parameter Converter and Calculator for Health Technology Assessment — ParCC-package

title: Run the ParCC Application — run_app

title: ParCC 1.4.0
text: Budget Impact Analysis – 5-year BIA framework population sizing, uptake curves, discounting (ISPOR Good Practice guidelines). PPP Currency Converter – Purchasing Power Parity conversion across 30 countries (World Bank 2022 ICP data), market exchange rate comparison, -CHOICE WTP threshold assessment (1x 3x GDP per capita).

title: ParCC 1.4.0
text: RR / RR – Convert Odds Ratios Relative Risks using baseline risk (Zhang & Yu 1998 method). Effect Size Transformations – Convert SMD log() using Chinn (2000) formula network meta-analysis preparation. Standalone NNT/NNH Calculator – Calculate Number Needed Treat four input modes: ARR, RR + baseline, + baseline, direct probabilities. Log-rank HR – Estimate Hazard Ratios published log-rank chi-square p-value using Peto approximation.

title: ParCC 1.4.0
text: Dirichlet Distribution – Fit Dirichlet parameters multinomial transition probabilities Markov models (via Gamma decomposition). Log-Logistic Survival – Third survival extrapolation option non-monotonic (hump-shaped) hazard support. Annuity / PV Stream Calculator – Present value recurring annual costs (ordinary annuity annuity due).

title: ParCC 1.4.0
text: Global Currency Selector – Switch INR, USD, EUR, GBP, JPY, BRL, THB, AUD, CAD, custom currencies. economic modules update automatically. Interactive Tool Overview – Collapsible accordion Home page clickable tool items navigate directly relevant module. Restructured Navbar – Explicit, user-friendly labels logical groupings (Convert, Survival Curves, Costs & Outcomes, Learn). Five new pkgdown vignettes worked examples.

title: ParCC 1.3.0
text: HR Converter Module – Convert Hazard Ratios time-dependent transition probabilities; compare multiple HRs simultaneously. Enhanced Output Panels – Step--step explanations, rendered MathJax formulas, literature citations every output panel. Batch HR Conversion – CSV upload bulk HR-based conversions alongside existing rate odds batch modes. pkgdown Documentation Site – Hosted https://drpakhare.github.io/ParCC/ vignettes core workflows.

title: ParCC 1.2.0
text: Initial public release core converters, survival extrapolation, PSA distributions, financial tools, ICER/NMB, VBP, diagnostics, Lab Notebook.
